items.question_text: When Bash runs a shell script, special parameter `$0` is set to the script file name rather than the shell name.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: If a shell script is run with no additional arguments, its positional parameters are automatically set to empty strings.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: A shell script can be made executable by using `chmod` to turn on the execute bit.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: When Bash finds an executable shell script through `PATH`, it always runs it in the current shell without creating a new Bash instance.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: In an `if` command, a zero status from the test command list causes the consequent command list to run.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: In an `if` command, `elif` branches are skipped unless the initial test command returned zero.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: If an `else` clause is present, it can run when the prior `if` or `elif` conditions do not succeed.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: The return status of an `if` command is always the status of its test command list.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: If no condition tested true, an `if` command returns zero.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: A `case` command returns failure by default when no pattern matches.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: If a `case` clause matches, the command's return status is the exit status of the last command-list executed.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: The `select` construct exists to make menu generation easier.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: If a `select` command omits the word list, it behaves as if no menu items exist at all.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: If `select` reads an empty line, it displays the menu words and prompt again.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: If `select` reads EOF, it completes and returns 1.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: If `select` reads a non-empty invalid value, it keeps the selection variable unchanged.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: The raw line read by `select` is stored in the `PS3` variable.

items.question_text: Inside a shell function named `ls`, using `command ls` can execute the external `ls` instead of recursing into the function.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: The `command` builtin can execute shell builtins or commands found through `PATH` lookup.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: If the requested command cannot be found, the `command` builtin returns status 0.

items.question_text: After a name is made readonly, later assignment or `unset` on that name is not allowed.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: Using `declare +r` removes the readonly attribute from a readonly variable.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
items.question_text: Inside a function, `declare` makes each declared name local unless `-g` is used.

items.reason: Question item was loaded from the structured curated profile and reviewed individually.
